subject: ap-comparative-government
theme: Economic globalization and natural resource endowments create both opportunities and political distortions in every course country — and how a government responds to these pressures, to demographic change, and to the demands of international organizations reveals the true balance between sovereignty and adaptation.

core_concepts.title: Economic Globalization and Liberalization
core_concepts.detail: Economic globalization is the increasing interconnection of national economies through trade, investment, and capital flows — with actors operating across political borders largely free of state restriction. Economic liberalization occurs when a state reduces its economic role by cutting subsidies and tariffs, privatizing state-owned industries, and opening to foreign direct investment. The IMF and World Bank promote liberalization by making loans conditional on structural adjustment programs (SAPs) — packages requiring privatization, tariff reduction, and subsidy cuts. Neoliberalism is the ideological framework behind liberalization — it calls for removing barriers on economic actors, privatizing state enterprises, and reducing regulation. The CED notes neoliberal policies have had mixed effects: they reduced inflation and increased national income in some countries, but also generated growing inequality, political corruption, and social tensions. Course countries of all regime types — democratic and authoritarian — have adopted liberalization policies.

core_concepts.title: Rentier States and the Resource Curse
core_concepts.detail: A rentier state is one that funds its government primarily from revenues derived from exporting natural resources — especially oil and gas — rather than taxing its citizens. Iran, Nigeria, and Russia are the three CED rentier state examples. Because governments do not need to tax citizens, they also feel less accountable to them — undermining democracy and transparency. The resource curse describes the paradox in which resource-rich countries often develop weaker economies, higher corruption, and less democracy than resource-poor ones. Specific resource curse outcomes identified in the CED include: lack of economic diversification, severe revenue volatility from world price fluctuations, currency overvaluation, increasing inequality between rich and poor, absence of democracy, increased corruption, and reduced government accountability. Russia's re-nationalization of oil and gas companies under Putin is a case study in using resource control as political power — the CED names Putin explicitly in this context (IEF-3.B.1d).

core_concepts.title: Country Responses to Global Market Forces
core_concepts.detail: Each course country has responded distinctively to global market forces. China: created special economic zones (SEZs) along the coast — designated regions where foreign investment and market rules apply, while the interior maintains state control; allows least private control of natural resources of any course country. Mexico: partial privatization of Pemex (state oil company) and increased competition in the oil sector; the maquiladora zones near the US border are export-assembly factories created under trade liberalization, especially NAFTA — they drove internal migration from south to north and increased regional disparity. Nigeria: the NNPC (Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation) operates in joint ventures with foreign multinationals to extract and sell oil; IMF structural adjustment programs and import substitution industrialization (ISI) policies (raising tariffs to build domestic industry) have both been tried. Russia: under Putin, reversed 1990s privatization by re-nationalizing oil and gas industries and imposing foreign investment limitations; uses resource revenues to fund political legitimacy. UK: allows the most private control of natural resources of any course country; Brexit represented a cultural and political backlash against EU economic integration and immigration.

core_concepts.title: International and Supranational Organizations
core_concepts.detail: International organizations influence course countries through financial conditionality and trade rules. The IMF and World Bank provide loans contingent on structural adjustment programs — countries must agree to privatize, cut subsidies, and reduce tariffs. Import substitution industrialization (ISI) is the counter-strategy some countries adopt to resist this: raising tariffs to protect domestic industries and reduce foreign dependency. Supranational organizations have authority that supersedes member-state sovereignty in certain areas: the EU governs trade and economic policy for members (UK departed via Brexit in 2020); the WTO adjudicates trade disputes and pressures members to liberalize trade; ECOWAS (Economic Community of West African States) includes Nigeria and applies pressure on West African states' trade policies. The key distinction for the exam: supranational organizations have binding authority over member states (they can compel action); international organizations like the IMF exert influence through conditionality (they make loans conditional on compliance, but cannot legally compel behavior).

core_concepts.title: Demographic Change and Social Policy
core_concepts.detail: Demographic changes — shifting populations, migration patterns, aging — create policy challenges affecting legitimacy. UK: positive net migration (more immigrants than emigrants) generates social and political tensions, fueling parties that oppose immigration; an aging population increases NHS costs while a shrinking working-age population faces higher tax burdens to fund universal health care. China: decades of the one-child policy (a demographic intervention limiting births) produced an aging population and gender imbalance; rural-to-urban and west-to-east migration driven by SEZs and economic opportunity created growing coastal urban populations. Mexico: NAFTA and maquiladora zone development drove south-to-north internal migration, deepening regional economic disparities between northern industrial zones and the rural south. Brain drain affects Iran and Nigeria: highly skilled individuals emigrate to escape government policies perceived as repressive, limiting, or corrupt — reducing both countries' human capital. Nigeria has unequal gender access to education between the predominantly Muslim north (lower female enrollment) and the south.

core_concepts.title: Measuring Development: HDI and Gini
core_concepts.detail: The AP exam regularly presents data tables and charts requiring students to interpret development indicators across the six course countries. The Human Development Index (HDI) is a composite measure of life expectancy, education levels, and income per capita — it captures human development beyond income alone. A high HDI does not guarantee democracy (China has improved its HDI significantly under authoritarian rule). The Gini coefficient (or Gini index) measures income inequality: a score closer to 0 indicates more equality; a score closer to 1 (or 100 on a 0–100 scale) indicates greater inequality. Mexico and Nigeria tend to have higher Gini scores than China or Russia. The Freedom House rating (Free / Partly Free / Not Free) measures political rights and civil liberties — a country can have high GDP and low Freedom House rating (China). A key exam skill: explain what patterns in a data table imply about the relationship between regime type, development, and inequality — and distinguish correlation from causation.

core_concepts.title: Globalization Challenges: Environment, Cultural Backlash, Sovereignty
core_concepts.detail: Globalization creates several political challenges beyond economics. Environmental degradation: rapid industrialization — especially in China — causes air pollution, water contamination, and urban sprawl that alienate citizens and force government policy responses (factory relocations, green technology subsidies, vehicle emission rules). Cultural backlash: Western cultural influences accompanying trade and investment provoke domestic resistance in Iran and Russia — fueling nationalist and populist movements that blame governments for cultural changes. Regime sovereignty is challenged when multinational corporations operate within a country's borders with competing economic interests (land rights, taxation, labor standards), when supranational rules constrain domestic policy, or when foreign governments impose economic sanctions (Iran, Russia) in response to human rights violations or geopolitical actions. Austerity measures — government spending cuts to reduce deficits under IMF pressure — have reduced social program funding in several course countries, undermining legitimacy.

exam_tip: Unit 5 features the most data-driven AP questions: be ready to read HDI, Gini, and oil-revenue tables and draw inferences rather than just describe them — the exam asks what patterns imply about regime stability, accountability, or inequality. Memorize the two spectrum anchors for resource control (UK = most private, China = least private) and the three rentier state examples (Iran, Nigeria, Russia), and know the specific resource curse outcomes the CED lists: lack of diversification, revenue volatility, corruption, weak accountability, and absence of democracy.
